ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No specific FoodEx2 code exists for uthappam; A0CKP (Pancake) is the closest parent category for this fermented rice-lentil pancake.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept identified for uthappam or South Indian fermented pancakes.

serving.natural_language_portion: One medium plain uthappam weighing approximately 100 g, served as a breakfast or snack item, typically accompanied by sambar and coconut chutney.

nutrient_source.title: Uttapam (Uthappam) — Nutritional composition per 100 g
nutrient_source.organization: ICMR-NIN (Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition), Hyderabad, India; cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for fermented rice-lentil preparations
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition) reports values for plain uttapam prepared from a standard rice:urad dal batter (3:1 ratio) without toppings. Values are consistent with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for similar fermented rice-lentil preparations. Serving size set to 100 g (one medium piece) for GL calculation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of South Indian fermented foods: Idli and Dosa analogues including Uttapam
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; supporting evidence from Shobana S et al. (2007) 'Glycaemic index of commonly consumed foods in India', British Journal of Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for plain uthappam was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 51 is estimated from the closest analogous fermented rice-lentil preparations (idli GI ~46–54, dosa GI ~50–55) as reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and Shobana et al. (2007, Br J Nutr). Plain uthappam uses the same batter as idli/dosa (rice:urad dal 3:1), fermented overnight, and is cooked on a griddle without oil addition beyond a light smear; its GI is expected to fall within the same low range. GL per serving = (51 × 28.5 g carbohydrate × 100 g serving) / 100 = 14.5.

hypertension.guidance: Plain uthappam contains approximately 210 mg sodium per 100 g, which is moderate; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and avoid high-sodium accompaniments such as salted chutneys or processed sambar mixes to keep total sodium intake within the recommended 1500–2000 mg/day.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~51) and moderate carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a glycemic index of approximately 51 (low) and a glycemic load of ~14.5 per 100 g serving, plain uthappam produces a moderate postprandial glucose response and can be included in a diabetes meal plan when portion-controlled and paired with high-fibre accompaniments such as sambar with vegetables.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat, cholesterol-free, and plant-based composition
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Plain uthappam is low in saturated fat (~0.4 g per 100 g), contains no cholesterol, and is made entirely from plant-based ingredients (rice and urad dal), making it a heart-friendly carbohydrate source when consumed without high-fat accompaniments such as ghee or coconut oil.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Plain uthappam (also spelled uttapam or uttappam) is a traditional South Indian fermented rice-and-lentil pancake made from a batter of soaked and ground rice and urad dal (black gram lentil) in approximately a 3:1 ratio, fermented overnight to develop a mild sour flavour and a light, porous texture. Unlike dosa, which is thin and crispy, uthappam is thick, soft, and spongy, resembling a thick pancake. The plain version contains no toppings, distinguishing it from onion uthappam or tomato uthappam. It is cooked on a hot griddle (tawa) with a minimal amount of oil until golden on both sides. Nutritionally, plain uthappam provides approximately 146 kcal per 100 g, with 28.5 g carbohydrate, 4.5 g protein, and 1.8 g fat. Its glycemic index is estimated at approximately 51 (low GI), making it suitable for individuals managing blood glucose. The fermentation process increases bioavailability of B vitamins and reduces phytic acid, improving mineral absorption. It is naturally gluten-free, vegan, and halal. In Singapore and Malaysia, uthappam is commonly served at Indian Muslim and South Indian restaurants as a breakfast or tiffin item, accompanied by sambar (a lentil-based vegetable stew) and coconut chutney. Clinically, it is a moderate-sodium food (~210 mg/100 g) and should be consumed with low-sodium accompaniments by individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease.
common_user_queries: Uthappam vs idli which is healthier

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) for plain uttapam, which is the closest approved primary source for this South Indian dish. GI is an analogous estimate based on idli and dosa data from the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Shobana et al. 2007), as no direct GI measurement for plain uthappam was found. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the analogous GI estimation and the adapted (not exact) nutrient match.
notes: 1. Cuisine tag in input JSON was 'Malaysian'; corrected to 'South Indian' as uthappam is a South Indian dish of Tamil Nadu origin, though it is widely served in Malaysia and Singapore at Indian restaurants. The field survey listing at 'New Shah Alam - Circular Road' is consistent with a Malaysian Indian restaurant context. 2. Multilingual names follow Singaporean dish rules (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) given the Singapore field survey source context and the dish's prevalence in Singapore's South Indian food scene. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero as plain uthappam is made from rice and lentils with no hydrogenated fats; confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN source. 4. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a plant-based food containing no animal products; confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN. 5. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN for plain uthappam without vegetable toppings. 6. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a plant-based food with no animal-derived ingredients; confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN. 7. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 — not reported in ICMR-NIN for plain uthappam without toppings. 8. vitamin_b6_mg is -1 — not separately reported in ICMR-NIN for this preparation. 9. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 — not reported in ICMR-NIN; naturally negligible in plant-based foods without fortification. 10. vitamin_e_mg is -1 — not reported in ICMR-NIN for this preparation. 11. selenium_mcg is -1 — not reported in ICMR-NIN for this preparation. 12. GI of 51 is an analogous estimate; a direct measurement study on plain uthappam would be required to upgrade confidence to high. 13. Price data from field survey (SGD 2.25) is preserved as context but not used in nutritional analysis.

Documented all -1 sentinel values (vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, selenium_mcg) with source-based reasons in notes.
